title: AI Infrastructure: Data Centre Electricity Demand Soars with Liquid Cooling Advancements | Ting Zou (Tina Tsou) posted on the topic | LinkedIn
text:
AI isn’t just algorithms, the infrastructure powering it is transforming data centres. • Data‑centre electricity demand is projected to soar: research suggests demand could increase by about 160% by 2030 as generative AI and cloud adoption grow. • High‑density AI racks are pushing well beyond 100 kW. NVIDIA’s GB200 NVL72 rack combines 36 Grace CPUs with 72 Blackwell GPUs and draws around 120 kW, requiring direct‑to‑chip liquid cooling. • Data‑centre capacity is constrained: primary market vacancy rates fell to around 1–1.6% in H1 2025 and in mature hubs vacancy has dropped below 1%, while global vacancy is roughly 6%. Efficiency is the only way to add capacity. • Liquid cooling is a game changer. Supermicro’s DLC‑2 solution captures up to 98% of heat, cuts data‑centre power consumption by up to 40%, reduces total cost of ownership by ~20% and operates quietly (~50 dB). It uses higher inlet temperatures, eliminating chillers and reducing water use by up to 40%. • Liquid‑ or immersion‑cooled systems deliver PUE near 1.05 (some micro‑jet systems approach 1.02), far better than air‑cooled alternatives, and energy savings often pay for the infrastructure in a year or two. Cooling is the new frontier in AI infrastructure. #AI #DataCenter #LiquidCooling #Sustainability

      Everyone is talking about #NVIDIA’s GPUs after the #GTC keynote. The bigger story is something else: #AI is becoming infrastructure. Jensen Huang described a future powered by AI factories, massive systems that convert energy, data, and compute into intelligence. These factories will manufacture something entirely new at industrial scale: predictions reasoning automation autonomous decision-making. But there’s an important layer in this conversation that often gets overlooked. AI factories run on and are delivered by #networks. Training models may happen inside clusters of GPUs, but real-world AI systems operate across infrastructure. Agents calling APIs. Inference running across clouds and edges. Applications interacting with users around the world. All of it moves across fiber routes, wireless spectrum, carrier networks, subsea cables, and data centers. In many ways, the economics of AI will ultimately depend on infrastructure efficiency: • cost per token • cost per inference • latency between AI systems and users • network paths between data, compute, and applications. If AI factories are the next generation of industrial production, then the global connectivity ecosystem becomes the logistics network that feeds those factories. At Connectbase, we’ve spent years building what we call the Location Graph for the Connected World — mapping the infrastructure, networks, and providers that power the digital economy. As AI demand scales, the ability to discover, buy, and automate connectivity between infrastructure and applications will become increasingly critical. The AI revolution won’t just be built in silicon. It will be built across the global connectivity ecosystem that moves intelligence everywhere it needs to go. The question is: are we building that infrastructure fast enough? #ecosystemledgrowth #connectivitymatters #locationtruth To view or add a comment, sign in

      The future of computing is going to be defined by efficiency, and the data already shows where things are heading. Over the last decade, advances in Reduced Instruction Set Computing designs and specialized AI accelerators have drastically increased the performance per watt of modern chips. Architectures built around parallel processing and optimized instruction pipelines allow AI workloads to run faster while using significantly less power than traditional general-purpose processors. Companies like NVIDIA are pushing this even further. Modern AI GPUs now deliver orders-of-magnitude improvements in energy efficiency per training operation compared to systems from just a decade ago. Data centers are already using AI to optimize power distribution, cooling, and workload scheduling - meaning the systems running AI are actively learning how to consume less energy while doing more work. Pair that with advances in renewable energy integration, edge computing, and intelligent power management, and you start to see the direction we’re going: autonomous computing infrastructure that can optimize its own energy usage in real time. It’s not about creating energy from nothing - it’s about maximizing every watt available through smarter hardware and smarter AI. We’re entering an era where AI doesn’t just run on computers - it helps design the next generation of computers. That feedback loop is where the real acceleration happens. I’m incredibly optimistic about being part of this shift someday. The intersection of AI, efficient chip architecture, and energy optimization is one of the most exciting frontiers in technology - and companies like NVIDIA are right at the center of it. The next decade of computing won’t just be more powerful. It will be smarter, more efficient, and built to sustain itself at scale and Jensen Huang is helping make it possible.⚡🤖NVIDIA To view or add a comment, sign in

      Delta Targets Next‑Generation AI Data Centers with 800 V DC Power Distribution and Advanced Liquid‑Cooling Solutions At Nvidia’s GTC 2026, Jensen Huang reminded us that raw GPU power alone won’t be enough for the AI boom — the way we deliver electricity and keep the chips cool must evolve together. Delta is answering that call with a bold plan: future AI data centres will run on 800 V DC power and use sophisticated liquid-cooling loops built right into the racks. By cutting conversion losses, shrinking cabling, and slashing the data-centre PUE to around 1.05, this approach promises lower operating costs, reduced carbon footprints, and more compute per square foot. The shift also means a new breed of engineers who understand both high-voltage systems and fluid dynamics. While the initial cap-ex is higher, the long-term savings — both economic and environmental — could be game-changing for AI-heavy industries. What do you think — will co-design of power and cooling become the new standard for AI factories?       What happens to your data center ROI when fetching data takes a tiny fraction of the energy? ⚡ Pushing data through traditional HBM requires firing up power-hungry controllers, leading to high power draw and hotter GPUs. In an era where energy availability is the primary constraint on AI scaling, this "thermal tax" is no longer sustainable. Memstak Specialty Memory is the breakthrough for sustainable AI scaling. By drastically reducing the energy needed for data movement, Memstak allows the entire accelerator to run cooler. ❄️ The Ripple Effect: Reduced Cooling Costs: A cooler GPU draws less power and puts less strain on data center cooling infrastructure. Performance Gains: Saved power is redirected to boost clock speeds, turning efficiency directly into velocity. We are fundamentally shifting the economics and the energy profile of the AI revolution.       Everyone talks about GPUs as the bottleneck of AI. But the real constraint is power. You can order accelerators from NVIDIA, AMD, or custom silicon from hyperscalers. What you can’t order overnight is gigawatts of electricity. Across the AI infrastructure landscape, a new class of operators is quietly locking in the most important asset in the stack: long-term power capacity. Several operators now control multi-gigawatt energy footprints dedicated to AI compute infrastructure: • IREN — ~4.5 GW • CoreWeave — ~3.1 GW • Nebius — ~2.0 GW • TeraWulf — ~643 MW • Cipher Mining — ~600 MW • Applied Digital — ~600 MW • Core Scientific — ~590 MW • Hut 8 — ~245 MW These aren’t just data centers. They’re effectively AI power plants and liquid-cooled, fiber-connected infrastructure built on long-term power purchase agreements designed to support hyperscale compute demand. And that demand is enormous. Cloud providers and AI companies are racing to deploy hundreds of thousands of GPUs to power training clusters, inference systems, and next-generation AI services. But here’s the structural tension: Power infrastructure is fixed and contracted for decades. Compute revenue is variable. GPU rental pricing can move quickly depending on supply cycles, new hardware releases, or shifts in demand. For operators managing gigawatts of power capacity, that creates a fundamental economic risk: long-term infrastructure commitments tied to short-term compute price volatility. As AI infrastructure scales globally, the industry will need better mechanisms for price discovery and risk management around the compute layer. At ByteStrike, we are focused on building financial infrastructure for the AI compute economy, including benchmark pricing indices and structured markets designed to bring transparency to GPU capacity pricing. Because when AI runs on gigawatts of power and billions in hardware, the economics of compute become just as important as the technology itself.       AI demand is pushing data centers to their limits, and power, not compute, is becoming the biggest constraint. A recent announcement from Utilidata and NexGen Cloud shows how the industry is addressing this challenge with AI-driven power management. Their Karman AI platform, running on a custom module developed with NVIDIA, dynamically manages GPU power consumption at the rack level inside data centers. The result? Operators can potentially unlock up to 50% more AI compute capacity using existing electrical infrastructure. Why this matters: • AI workloads require massive GPU clusters • Electrical capacity and grid limits are now the bottleneck • Many facilities have “stranded” power capacity that can't safely be used today AI-powered energy orchestration can reclaim that unused headroom by monitoring and balancing power usage in real time across racks and workloads. There’s also a Michigan connection here: Utilidata recently opened an innovation lab in Ann Arbor, putting the region right in the middle of next-generation infrastructure innovation. As AI data centers scale globally, expect to see more innovation focused not just on chips and software, but on how we intelligently manage power, cooling, and infrastructure inside the data center itself. For IT and infrastructure leaders, the takeaway is clear: The future of AI capacity may depend as much on power orchestration as it does on GPUs. #AI #DataCenters #MichiganTech #DigitalInfrastructure #EnergyInnovation #NVIDIA #AIInfrastructure To view or add a comment, sign in

      🚨 AI’s Next Bottleneck Isn’t Compute. It’s Cooling. The new generation of AI chips — like NVIDIA Blackwell GB200 from NVIDIA — are incredibly powerful. But they come with a challenge: Heat. Lots of it. Traditional air cooling that worked for earlier data centers is reaching its physical limits. As GPU density increases, temperatures rise, and efficiency drops. That’s why the industry is rapidly shifting toward liquid cooling. 💡 Liquid cooling allows: • Higher compute density • Better energy efficiency • Stable performance for AI workloads In simple terms... As AI models grow bigger, cooling infrastructure becomes mission-critical. Just like during a gold rush, the real winners are often the ones providing the infrastructure. While everyone talks about AI models and chips, a quiet opportunity may lie in companies enabling: ⚙️ Data center infrastructure 💧 Liquid cooling systems 🔌 Power and thermal management Because in the AI era, cooling may become just as important as computing. #ArtificialIntelligence #DataCenters #NVIDIA #AIInfrastructure #Semiconductors #Megatrends #InvestingInsights#EnggandEPC#cables#HVAC To view or add a comment, sign in

      Data centers are grappling with one of AI's most expensive headaches, and Niv-AI might have just cracked it. The startup just emerged from stealth with $12M in funding to tackle GPU power surges during dynamic AI workloads. When an Nvidia H100 cluster jumps between tasks, power demand isn’t smooth, it spikes—sometimes by 50% above baseline in milliseconds. For hyperscalers, this variability is a massive problem. It caps how many GPUs you can fit in a building before hitting electrical ceilings, stranding millions in unused infrastructure. Niv-AI’s solution? Real-time measurement and power management to predict and smooth out these spikes, effectively unlocking more compute capacity at the same power budget. Here’s why this matters: The AI gold rush isn’t just about securing GPUs anymore, it's about making the most of the ones you have. With next-gen chips like Nvidia Blackwell demanding even more power, operational constraints are becoming the new scalability bottleneck. The implications span everything from architecture design to cloud pricing models. If solutions like Niv-AI deliver, they could force a rethinking of AI infrastructure economics. Which brings the bigger question: As our workloads scale and variability intensifies, how should we balance hardware innovation with operational efficiency?       📡 BRINGING AI INFERENCE TO THE EDGE AT SCALE HPE launched the AI Grid at NVIDIA GTC today, and it represents a meaningful step forward in how AI gets deployed beyond the centralized data center. The core idea: any site with power and a network connection can become part of a unified, distributed AI inference system. The HPE AI Grid connects AI factories to regional sites and far-edge locations - telco base stations, enterprise branch offices, retail environments - and treats them all as part of one intelligent system with zero-touch provisioning and automated security. The use cases are significant. Real-time AI applications in healthcare, manufacturing, and public services often cannot send data to a central cloud for latency or compliance reasons. Inference needs to happen where the data lives. The HPE AI Grid provides the architecture to make that viable at scale. What is notable in the announcement is the partnership with Comcast, which announced AI field trials on its distributed network infrastructure - testing small language models on HPE ProLiant servers with NVIDIA GPUs to deliver AI services to small businesses via their existing cable infrastructure. That last detail is easy to miss. Comcast running AI inference on its cable network is a glimpse of a future where AI capacity is as distributed as internet access itself - baked into the infrastructure that businesses already pay for. The infrastructure for democratized AI access may be closer than it looks. #HPE #NVIDIA #EdgeAI #AIInfrastructure #DistributedAI To view or add a comment, sign in
